Key Instruments: 2 TV cameras (one for navigation, one for science), X-ray fluorescence spectrometer, penetrometer, laser retroreflector, radiation detectors, odometer
Objectives: First robotic rover on another world; soil analysis; surface photography; test remote control from Earth
Notes: Controlled from Earth by 5-person team; 4-level pause system for obstacles; soil density 0.8 g/cm³; FIRST successful lunar rover

Mobility System: 4 wheels (wire mesh tires, independent suspension, 0.25 HP motor each)
Key Instruments: TV camera (remote controlled from Earth), sample collection bags, hand tools, seatbelts, footrests, tool carrier
Objectives: Extend astronaut range for sampling; geological traverse; test crewed surface mobility
Notes: Max speed 13 km/h; carried 2 astronauts + 490 kg payload; folded to fit in LM; FIRST crewed planetary rover

Key Instruments: TV camera, sampling tools, experiment packages
Objectives: Geological traverse of Descartes Highlands; sample collection
Notes: Young and Duke drove; collected 95.7 kg samples

Key Instruments: TV camera, sampling tools, experiment packages
Objectives: Final Apollo lunar exploration; deepest drilling (3m); traverse Taurus-Littrow valley
Notes: Max speed 17.7 km/h (lunar surface speed record); Cernan and Schmitt; 110.5 kg samples; LAST crewed lunar rover to date

Key Instruments: 3 TV cameras (1 high-res, 2 navigation), X-ray fluorescence spectrometer, solar X-ray telescope, magnetometer, radiation detector, odometer, laser retroreflector
Objectives: Extended lunar exploration; traverse Le Monnier; soil and surface analysis; solar X-ray observations
Notes: Improved over Lunokhod 1; covered more distance; solar X-ray telescope; heat-induced dust issue ended mission; laser retroreflector still used

Key Instruments: Panoramic camera, VIS/NIR imaging spectrometer, active particle-induced X-ray spectrometer (APXS), ground-penetrating radar (2 frequencies: 60MHz and 500MHz)
Objectives: First Chinese lunar rover; surface composition analysis; subsurface structure study; extreme environment survival test
Notes: FIRST lunar rover in 40 years; mechanical control issues initially; survived 3 lunar nights; ground-penetrating radar found 9 distinct subsurface layers; 'Yutu' means Jade Rabbit

Key Instruments: Panoramic camera, VIS/NIR imaging spectrometer, APXS, ground-penetrating radar (3 frequencies), lunar neutron dosimeter, neutral atom detector
Objectives: Far side exploration; composition analysis; subsurface structure; radiation environment; neutron measurements
Notes: LONGEST operating lunar rover EVER (7+ years); 40+ lunar days survived; discovered gel-like substance; found new mineral Changesite-(Y); relayed via Queqiao

Landing Site: Shiv Shakti Point (69.37°S, 32.35°E) near south pole
Key Instruments: APXS (Alpha Particle X-ray Spectrometer), LIBS (Laser-Induced Breakdown Spectroscope)
Objectives: First successful rover near lunar south pole; elemental composition analysis; search for water ice indicators
Notes: Detected sulfur, aluminium, calcium, iron, chromium, titanium, manganese, silicon, oxygen; surface temperature 70°C recorded; entered sleep mode for lunar night but didn't wake; 'Pragyan' means Wisdom in Sanskrit

Key Instruments: APXS, LIBS
Objectives: South pole exploration; elemental analysis
Notes: Lost when Vikram lander crashed Sep 6, 2019

Key Instruments: Wide-angle camera, thermometer, radiation monitor, communication system (relayed LEV-2 data)
Objectives: Technology demonstration; autonomous surface exploration; test small rover mobility; relay communications
Notes: FIRST Japanese lunar surface rover; hopped across surface; autonomous navigation; successfully relayed LEV-2 images to Earth

Mobility System: Transformable: sphere → 2 hemispherical wheels + tail stabilizer
Key Instruments: 2 cameras (front and rear), temperature sensor
Objectives: World's smallest and lightest lunar rover; autonomous exploration; transformable mobility demonstration; image SLIM lander
Notes: WORLD'S smallest lunar rover (80mm diameter sphere); transformable design; took iconic image of SLIM lander face-down; FIRST fully autonomous lunar exploration; published in Science Robotics

Key Instruments: Cameras
Objectives: Surface imaging for ispace M1
Notes: Lost when Hakuto-R M1 crashed April 2023

Key Instruments: Camera, infrared spectrometer
Objectives: Far side surface imaging; sample context documentation; spectrometry
Notes: Small rover deployed by Chang'e 6; took images of lander and surface; surprise announcement (initially secret); helped document sampling site

Key Instruments: CAMS (microscopic camera), Langmuir probe, thermal camera
Objectives: UAE's first lunar mission; study lunar surface properties; plasma environment; thermal characteristics
Notes: FIRST Arab lunar rover attempt; lost with Hakuto-R M1 crash; Rashid 2 planned

Key Instruments: Camera, communication system
Objectives: Micro rover for surface exploration; ispace's second rover attempt
Notes: Lost when Hakuto-R M2 crashed June 2025

Key Instruments: RESOURCE camera (stereo), AstroAnt (miniature rover), thermal sensors
Objectives: Commercial lunar surface exploration; autonomous prospecting; technology demonstration
Notes: Deployed on IM-2; limited operations due to lander tipping on side; FIRST commercial US lunar rover on surface

Key Instruments: Thermal sensors, magnetometers
Objectives: Miniature rover for detailed surface measurements; swarm robotics concept
Notes: World's smallest lunar rover class; rides on MAPP; limited ops due to lander issue

Key Instruments: Puli Lunar Water Snooper (PLWS), Lunar Radiometer (LRAD), camera
Objectives: Hop into permanently shadowed craters; search for water ice; demonstrate hopping mobility
Notes: Designed to make multiple 100m+ hops; couldn't deploy properly due to lander orientation

Key Instruments: Camera, temperature sensors
Objectives: Demonstrate walking mobility on lunar surface; alternative to wheeled rovers
Notes: FIRST walking lunar rover attempt; limited ops due to lander issue

Key Instruments: Dual camera system (wide and narrow FOV)
Objectives: Image the Odysseus lander from surface; technology demonstration
Notes: Deployed ~4m from Odysseus; captured landing site images; CubeSat-class surface payload

Key Instruments: Neutron Spectrometer System (NSS), Near-Infrared Volatiles Spectrometer System (NIRVSS), Mass Spectrometer Observing Lunar Operations (MSolo), 1-meter drill (TRIDENT)
Objectives: Map water ice distribution at south pole; characterize volatiles; determine accessibility for ISRU; support Artemis crewed missions
Notes: CANCELLED July 2024, REVIVED Sept 2025 with Blue Origin; car-sized; will search for ice in permanently shadowed regions; CRITICAL for Artemis

Key Instruments: Cameras, communication systems, cargo carriers, scientific instrument mounts
Objectives: Transport astronauts on lunar surface; remote operation between crewed missions; cargo transport; support extended EVAs
Notes: Will be delivered before Artemis V; operates remotely when crew absent; 800 kg cargo capacity; survive lunar night; 3 companies developing (IM selected)

Key Instruments: Panoramic camera, imaging spectrometer, ground-penetrating radar, APXS, LIBS
Objectives: Search for water ice in permanently shadowed regions; characterize south pole environment; support ILRS planning
Notes: Part of complex Chang'e 7 mission with orbiter, lander, and hopper; high-precision landing support

Key Instruments: Cameras, spectrometers (TBD)
Objectives: Pakistan's first lunar rover; international cooperation under ILRS framework; technology demonstration
Notes: Part of Chang'e 8 international payload; 11 countries participating in Chang'e 8

Key Instruments: Cameras, spectrometers (TBD)
Objectives: Turkey's first lunar rover; ISRU technology demonstration
Notes: International cooperation under ILRS

Key Instruments: Multiple spectrometers, cameras, drill, ground-penetrating radar
Objectives: Long-range lunar exploration; extensive surface analysis; support future Russian lunar base
Notes: Most ambitious Russian rover; mid-size category; part of Luna 30 lander mission

Key Instruments: Enhanced cameras, Langmuir probe, thermal camera, ground-penetrating radar
Objectives: Second UAE lunar rover; improved design; search for water ice; study plasma environment
Notes: Lessons from Rashid failure incorporated; may fly on CLPS or international mission

Key Instruments: Camera, spectrometer, ice-prospecting instruments
Objectives: Canada's first lunar rover; ice prospecting at south pole; technology demonstration for Artemis
Notes: Canadian contribution to Artemis program; rides on CLPS lander

Key Instruments: Cameras, temperature sensors, communication between rovers
Objectives: Demonstrate autonomous multi-rover operations; swarm robotics; distributed exploration
Notes: Three rovers operating cooperatively without direct Earth control; technology demonstration

Key Instruments: Cameras, neutron spectrometer, ice-detection instruments
Objectives: Small autonomous polar rover; ice prospecting; demonstrate long-range autonomous navigation
Notes: Scalable design for future missions; high autonomy level

Key Instruments: Camera, sensors
Objectives: Small commercial lunar rover; low-cost lunar mobility services
Notes: Japanese commercial rover; multiple units planned

Key Instruments: Camera, temperature sensors
Objectives: Demonstrate walking mobility; access terrain impossible for wheeled rovers
Notes: Unique spider-like walking mechanism; alternative mobility concept

Key Instruments: Camera, spectrometer
Objectives: Google Lunar X Prize competition rover; technology development
Notes: Developed for GLXP; evolved into ispace landing program; never flew

Key Instruments: Camera, basic sensors (modular)
Objectives: Standardized small rover platform; mass-producible; modular payload
Notes: 2 kg class; scalable; intended for widespread deployment on multiple missions

Key Instruments: Drill (1.5m), neutron spectrometer, ground-penetrating radar, camera suite
Objectives: Japan-India joint mission; water ice exploration; ISRU demonstration; prepare for human presence
Notes: Joint JAXA-ISRO mission; evolved from earlier Resource Prospector concepts; significant international cooperation
